title: Geese on a smallholding
subtitle: What keeping a small flock of geese actually involves: grazing, housing, behaviour, and table use.
excerpt: Geese are hardy, largely self-sufficient on grass, and serve several useful functions on a smallholding: grazing, egg production in spring, table birds at Christmas, and an effective early-warning system for the yard. They are also loud, opinionated, and completely unsuitable for small urban gardens. Getting the match right is the starting point.
sourceNotes: Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive: geese chapter covering breeds, management, and table use. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Geese (2010), gov.uk. John Seymour, The New Complete Book of Self-Sufficiency, geese section.

glossaryTerms.definition: A male goose. One gander is typically kept per three to five geese. A gander is territorial during breeding season in early spring and will display aggressively toward strangers and animals entering his perceived territory.
A young goose from hatch to approximately 8 weeks of age, while still carrying down rather than full feather.

The proportion of a goose's diet that can come from grazed grass. An adult goose can obtain much of its nutrient needs from good quality pasture, reducing purchased feed significantly compared to chickens. Grass must be short and actively growing; poached, wet, or rank grass provides little nutrition.

Geese are seasonal layers, producing eggs from January or February through to early summer. Most breeds lay 20 to 40 eggs per season, far fewer than chickens. The eggs are large, with a strong yolk, and are used in cooking and baking.

content.text: Geese are the most grass-dependent domestic poultry. A small flock of four to six geese on half an acre of well-managed pasture will supplement their own diet substantially in spring and summer, requiring only grain as a top-up when the grass is short or the growing season is over. What they need is space, water, secure overnight housing, and a gander willing to do his job as the flock's sentinel.

For the smallholder, the practical choice comes down to size, temperament, and table weight. Toulouse and Embden are the standard heavy table breeds: Toulouse is quieter, Embden can be more assertive. Chinese and Brown Chinese geese are lighter, productive layers for their size, and very vocal. The Pilgrim is a sexable breed (males white, females grey) that suits mixed farmyard settings and has a calmer disposition than Chinese. Brecon Buff is a good dual-purpose choice for UK smallholdings and is less aggressive than some commercial strains.

for geese is notably better than any other domestic fowl, but the grass must be available and of reasonable quality. A minimum of 40 square metres per bird is the standard guidance; on good pasture in a wet summer they can graze for the majority of their nutrition from May through September. In winter, when grass stops growing, supplement with wheat and hay to maintain condition.

Rotate the grazing area if possible to prevent fouling concentration and poaching. Geese defecate continuously and heavily; a patch of ground kept at high density through winter will be bare and waterlogged. Divide the area with moveable electric netting into two or three sections and rotate on a two-week cycle.

Geese do not need elaborate housing. A simple three-sided shelter with a solid back and roof, well-bedded with straw, works for most of the year. During the laying season in late winter and early spring, a fully enclosed house with a pop-hole is more important: geese will lay eggs in the field if they do not have a secure indoor nest site, and eggs in a field at night will attract foxes. The house must be fox-proof; a badger or fox can deal with a loose door latch. A flat board on top of a concrete block is sufficient weight for most door systems.

Geese require water deep enough to submerge their heads and clear their nostrils. A rubber bucket or low trough at least 20 cm deep is the minimum; a paddling pool or shallow pond is better. They do not require access to swimming water for health, but they are considerably calmer and in better feather condition with it. A concrete or rubberised pond that can be drained and cleaned weekly prevents algal and bacterial build-up that open water in summer can accumulate.

in breeding season is territorial toward people and other animals. He will hiss, charge, and beat with his wings. Children and small dogs need supervising in the goose yard from January to May. A gander that becomes notably dangerous after repeated handling can be caught, penned separately, or culled; some individuals are simply not manageable around people. This is a breed and individual variation, not a training issue.

runs from January or February to early summer, producing 20 to 40 large eggs per goose per season. The eggs are excellent in cooking: the yolk is rich and the white sets firmer than a hen's egg, which makes them well suited to cakes, pasta, and omelettes. They are not well suited to long-term storage as their shells are porous.

For table birds, geese reach slaughter weight at 24 to 28 weeks for a Christmas kill on birds hatched in spring. Heavy breeds such as Toulouse reach 8 to 12 kg liveweight; the dressed weight is roughly 70% of liveweight. Plucking a goose is more work than a chicken because the feathers are dense and the down is commercially valuable if collected separately. Dry-plucking takes 30 to 45 minutes for an experienced hand.

attrs.title: Goslings from an incubator or broody hen

content.text: Goose eggs require 28 to 30 days of incubation (Toulouse and heavy breeds), with the egg turned daily and humidity at 65 to 70%. A Muscovy duck makes a willing and reliable broody for goose eggs; a chicken hen will sit them but tends to give up before the hatch date.
go onto grass within two weeks if the weather is dry and mild; earlier access to short grass is the single most beneficial thing for their development.
